segments.title: Choosing museum ships for the history they can explain
segments.summary: A famous ship is not automatically the best preservation choice: the question is what history a visitor could learn from her.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses HMS Dreadnought to set a demanding preservation test. Although she was the first of her type, he argues that her short operational story and absence from Jutland limit the range of history she could illuminate. The discussion instead uses the ship to challenge the common idea that the Anglo-German naval race began in 1905: he places its roots in the 1880s and 1890s and connects later battleship design to wider British, Japanese, Italian, American, and German competition.

evidence.note: Dreadnought is assessed against the historical story a preserved ship could tell, including the longer naval arms race.

segments.title: When a surviving representative already tells the story
segments.summary: Existing survivors such as Mikasa, USS Texas, and HMCS Haida affect which lost ships would add the most to a museum landscape.
segments.body: The comparison with Royal Sovereign-class battleships introduces another preservation criterion: whether a closely comparable example survives elsewhere. Dr. Clarke prefers not to define nineteenth-century battleships merely as pre-dreadnoughts, and notes that Mikasa already provides a representative survivor. He makes the same practical comparison with USS Texas and HMCS Haida, arguing that a museum collection gains most when it preserves a type or story otherwise missing.

evidence.note: The transcript weighs Royal Sovereign against existing representatives and names Texas and Haida as useful comparisons.

segments.title: A quinquereme could settle arguments about ancient rowing
segments.summary: A surviving quinquereme would let historians test competing reconstructions of how large ancient warships arranged their oarsmen.
segments.body: The video treats a quinquereme as valuable less for a single national narrative than for the physical evidence it could provide. Dr. Clarke rejects the Victorian-style image of many vertically stacked banks of independently worked oars as impractical, favouring an arrangement in which multiple men share the higher oars. He argues that examples from Roman, Carthaginian, Egyptian, and Greek traditions would turn a long-running reconstruction debate into a question that surviving hull evidence could help answer.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke explains why surviving Mediterranean multi-level galleys would clarify the disputed rowing arrangement.

segments.title: Zheng He's treasure ships and the problem of scale
segments.summary: The scale, rig, and construction of Zheng He's treasure ships remain contested because the surviving evidence does not settle what the great voyages required.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke highlights the vast range of proposed dimensions for the treasure ships and asks whether a conventional junk form could have been scaled to those sizes. He links the uncertainty to reported fleet strengths, large crews, the voyages to Africa, and historical units of measurement whose length varied by place and time. A surviving example would offer a way to test the claims against the structure, rigging, and capacity of the vessel itself rather than relying only on written accounts and reconstructions.

evidence.note: The transcript surveys competing treasure-ship sizes, fleet figures, Chinese measurements, and voyage evidence.

segments.title: Korean turtle ships and the question of early naval armour
segments.summary: Replicas keep the turtle-ship story visible, but they cannot resolve whether their protection used plates, spikes, painted wood, or a mixture of them.
segments.body: The turtle ships associated with Admiral Yi Sun-sin are presented as historically important vessels whose exact construction remains uncertain. Dr. Clarke distinguishes the many modern replicas from physical evidence of the original ships and focuses on the dispute over iron protection: plates, spikes, locally protected areas, or wood made to resemble iron. The ships helped Korea resist Japanese invasion, but their connection with a commander whom the monarchy regarded as a political threat also helps explain why the vessels were not carefully retained. Resolving the armour question would affect how historians understand Korean naval warfare and the longer history of protected fighting ships.

evidence.note: The transcript connects turtle-ship construction debates to Yi Sun-sin's campaigns and the history of naval armour.

segments.title: Why written accounts cannot replace a surviving hull
segments.summary: The turtle-ship armour dispute becomes a lesson in source criticism: vivid historical language can preserve an impression while omitting the technical detail historians need.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses descriptions of iron on turtle-ship roofs to show why written testimony must be interpreted rather than treated as an engineering drawing. An author might mean plates, spikes, protected patches, or simply a surface that looked metallic, yet consider the distinction irrelevant to the battle story being recorded. Historians therefore compare perspectives, test claims against other evidence, and remain alert to what a writer chose not to explain. A surviving original hull would narrow the range of interpretation in a way that even numerous replicas and evocative accounts cannot.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke explains how imagery, omitted technical detail, and differing perspectives complicate the use of written accounts as evidence.

segments.title: Great Michael at the start of a new European naval race
segments.summary: Scotland's Great Michael helps trace the transition from medieval fighting ships toward the naval competition that produced ships of the line.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke places Great Michael at the beginning of a major change in European naval technology and rivalry. Built as a Scottish flagship, then sold to France after the death of James IV, she may have been involved in the operations surrounding the loss of Mary Rose; the video treats that connection cautiously. Her size prevented her from using the principal French naval dockyard, exposing the ship to weaker maintenance arrangements in a commercial port. Her larger historical importance is as a stimulus to English responses such as Great Harry and to the design evolution that eventually produced the ships of the line.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke links Great Michael, Great Harry, the Scottish-French transfer, and the changing European naval design race.

segments.title: How geography and maintenance helped destroy Great Michael
segments.summary: Great Michael's loss illustrates how an exceptional warship can decay when dock size, distance, and divided responsibility make routine inspection difficult.
segments.body: Great Michael was reportedly too large for the relevant French naval dockyard and had to remain in a commercial port some distance away. Dr. Clarke stresses that ports which appear close on a map may be separated by geography that makes travel easiest by sea. Naval officials responsible for many ships would visit infrequently and might depend on local reports from people with less warship expertise or weaker incentives. The episode turns the ship's disappearance into a lesson about infrastructure and maintenance institutions, not merely the fragility of a wooden hull.

evidence.note: The transcript links Great Michael's deterioration to dock limits, port geography, infrequent inspection, and reliance on local oversight.

segments.title: HMS Implacable: the last lost third-rate
segments.summary: The French-built former Duguay-Trouin survived for generations after Trafalgar, making her 1949 scuttling a major lost opportunity to preserve a third-rate.
segments.body: The video identifies HMS Implacable as a rare chance to preserve the most common type of battleship of the age of sail. Formerly the French Duguay-Trouin, she fought at Trafalgar, was captured by the Royal Navy at the Battle of Cape Ortegal, and remained afloat into the twentieth century. Dr. Clarke argues that a French-built, British-captured third-rate could have anchored a major maritime museum outside Portsmouth, illustrating both the ship type and the transnational history of the Napoleonic wars. Her 1949 scuttling is especially stark because the vessel survived for roughly 149 years before the decision was made to dispose of her.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Implacable's French origins, Trafalgar service, Royal Navy capture, long survival, and 1949 disposal.

segments.title: Implacable and the value beyond a preservation bill
segments.summary: The decision to save a historic ship should weigh tourism, education, and cultural value against the immediate cost entered on a government ledger.
segments.body: Implacable becomes the video's clearest example of the difference between knowing a ship's price and understanding its value. Dr. Clarke acknowledges that preservation would have required scarce money in 1949, when Britain was only four years beyond the Second World War. He nevertheless argues that a third-rate with French and British history could have drawn visitors from around the world and anchored the economy of a maritime museum. The comparison with Portsmouth and HMS Victory presents preservation as a long-term cultural and financial investment rather than a cost with no return.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke contrasts immediate preservation cost with the educational, tourism, and local-economic value a third-rate museum ship could create.

segments.title: HMS Carcass: bomb vessel, Arctic expedition, and Nelson
segments.summary: HMS Carcass would preserve both the unusual engineering of a bomb vessel and a vivid Arctic chapter from Nelson's early career.
segments.body: Carcass began as a bomb vessel, a specialised wooden sailing-warship form that is not otherwise represented by a preserved ship in the video. On an Arctic expedition she carried the midshipman Horatio Nelson, whose encounter with a polar bear becomes a reminder of how contingent later naval history could be. Dr. Clarke sees the vessel as small enough for a regional harbour yet rich enough in design, exploration, and Nelson-era history to reward preservation.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Carcass as a bomb vessel, her Arctic voyage, and Nelson's polar-bear incident.

segments.title: Gloire and the French push into ironclad warfare
segments.summary: Preserving Gloire would make the French role in the ironclad transition as visible as the later and more famous HMS Warrior.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that Gloire is essential to understanding the moment when France pressed naval technology forward and Britain responded with Warrior and Black Prince. In his telling, Warrior's scale and later survival have eclipsed Gloire, even though the French ship helps explain the competition that produced Britain's answer. Seeing the two in different ports across the Channel would allow visitors to compare a French development from Crimean War-era armoured batteries with the British overmatch it provoked.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts Gloire with Warrior and Black Prince as competing expressions of the ironclad transition.

segments.title: HMS Devastation and the mastless battleship
segments.summary: The first mastless battleship would fill a gap in preserved warships from the era between sail and the later dreadnoughts.
segments.body: HMS Devastation is selected as a practical way to explain a battleship era with few surviving examples. Dr. Clarke calls her the first mastless battleship and contrasts that relatively clear story with the more complicated task of representing contemporary cruiser development with a single hull. He also sees her as evidence that the late nineteenth-century naval race was wider than an Anglo-German contest alone.

evidence.note: The transcript presents Devastation as the first mastless battleship and a missing representative of her period.

segments.title: HMS Tiger and the missing battlecruiser type
segments.summary: No battlecruiser survives today, and Dr. Clarke makes HMS Tiger the representative he would most want visitors to be able to explore.
segments.body: Tiger is chosen over the narrator's personal favourite, Renown, because she offers a clearer historical example of the battlecruiser as built and operated before the Second World War. The video imagines how keeping her through the 1930s might have eased Royal Navy refits and perhaps led to a modernised wartime role, while recognising that those are counterfactual possibilities. Her late survival after sale for breaking also shows that scrapping was a drawn-out process rather than an immediate disappearance. The central point is simpler: a large, graceful battlecruiser in a British harbour would preserve a naval type now entirely absent from the museum fleet.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke compares Tiger with Renown, notes the absence of surviving battlecruisers, and considers Tiger's potential later utility.

segments.title: How keeping Tiger could have unlocked battlecruiser refits
segments.summary: The Royal Navy had yard capacity to refit its surviving battlecruisers, but too few available hulls to release more than one ship at a time.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses Tiger to separate industrial capacity from operational availability. The Royal Navy possessed dockyard space for more prewar modernization work, but could not let the ready battlecruiser force fall below two ships while Hood, Renown, or Repulse entered refit. Retaining Tiger could have supplied the coverage needed to move the other ships through the yards faster. The counterfactual shows how one additional hull can affect fleet readiness even when it is not the newest or most capable unit.

evidence.note: The transcript explains that yard space existed, but battlecruiser coverage requirements prevented multiple refits before the Second World War.

segments.title: HMS Warspite and Britain's lost battleship generation
segments.summary: Warspite's long First World War-to-Second World War career made her a powerful candidate to represent a whole generation of British capital ships.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke presents Warspite as a survivor whose wrecking near Prussia Cove made the loss especially painful. Her service would have allowed a preserved ship to connect the First World War, the interwar period, and the Second World War through one hull. He stresses that Britain retained no battleship from that generation, leaving museum visitors with only smaller vessels and displays to interpret a navy that shaped much of the period's history.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Warspite's proposed preservation site, disposal, and value as a representative British battleship.

segments.title: HMS Rodney as a Washington Treaty battleship
segments.summary: Rodney would preserve the unusually constrained design logic of the Washington naval treaties as well as her operational role in the sinking of Bismarck.
segments.body: The video distinguishes an early Washington Treaty battleship from later treaty-era American survivors and identifies Rodney and Nelson as unusually valuable examples. Rodney's wartime service, including the destruction of Bismarck, adds an operational story to the treaty history embodied in her design. Dr. Clarke argues that her comparatively younger hull and one-war service made her a plausible museum candidate had Britain already chosen to preserve Warspite.

evidence.note: The transcript presents Rodney as a rare Washington Treaty example and recalls her Bismarck service.

segments.title: USS Saratoga: battlecruiser conversion and nuclear-test victim
segments.summary: USS Saratoga could have shown visitors both an early American battlecruiser design and a major conversion carrier, but was used in a postwar nuclear test.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke sees Saratoga as a stronger representative conversion carrier than HMS Furious because her former battlecruiser hull allows the design transition to be read more clearly. He also emphasises the carrier's extensive Second World War service and the range of operations it could have illustrated in a museum. Her destruction in the atomic tests is presented as an avoidable loss because other target ships, including captured German and Japanese vessels, were available.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Saratoga's conversion-carrier value, wartime service, and nuclear-test loss.

segments.title: USS Enterprise as the symbol of the US Navy's Pacific war
segments.summary: Enterprise was one of only three US carriers to begin the Second World War and survive it, making her loss a missed chance to interpret the American Pacific war effort.
segments.body: The discussion treats Enterprise as a living symbol of the US Navy's wartime experience rather than merely another carrier. Dr. Clarke contrasts her with USS Ranger, whose survival would not tell the same useful story, and notes that Enterprise remained available for years before breaking-up began. A preserved ship could have displayed aircraft from several periods, hosted historical events, and made the scale of carrier warfare tangible to visitors.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Enterprise's wartime survival, status beside Saratoga, and eventual scrapping after years alongside the pier.

segments.title: HMS Illustrious from Taranto to the British Pacific Fleet
segments.summary: HMS Illustrious would represent the Royal Navy's armoured carriers and a Second World War career spanning Taranto, Malta, the Indian Ocean, and the Pacific.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke makes Illustrious the carrier most worth saving for Britain because no British carrier survives as a museum ship. Her operations are used to connect the Fleet Air Arm's attack at Taranto, the struggle to sustain Malta, and service in the Indian Ocean and British Pacific Fleet. Taranto also gave British naval aviation a highly visible success after the politically fraught action at Mers-el-Kebir had failed to win the service comparable public credit. The ship therefore offers more than one battle narrative: she is a route into the Royal Navy's changing use and reputation of carrier air power across several theatres.

evidence.note: The transcript links Illustrious to Taranto, Malta, multiple theatres, and the absence of any preserved British carrier.

segments.title: From Mers-el-Kebir to Taranto in Fleet Air Arm reputation
segments.summary: Taranto gave the Fleet Air Arm an affirmative demonstration of carrier striking power after Mers-el-Kebir had been overshadowed by the politics of attacking the French fleet.
segments.body: The video distinguishes military effectiveness from the public meaning attached to an operation. British naval aviation had already acted at Mers-el-Kebir, but Dr. Clarke describes that attack as a necessary and distasteful response to lost confidence in the French government's assurances after the fall of France. Its political burden made it a poor showcase for the Fleet Air Arm. Taranto instead allowed naval aviators to demonstrate their value through a clear offensive success against an enemy fleet, making Illustrious central to the service's institutional story.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke contrasts the political meaning of Mers-el-Kebir with Taranto's value as a visible Fleet Air Arm success.

segments.title: HMS Hermes, later INS Viraat, and a lost Anglo-Indian museum ship
segments.summary: The long-lived carrier HMS Hermes, later India's INS Viraat, could have preserved a rare link from postwar British carrier design through the Falklands to Indian naval service.
segments.body: Hermes is presented as an unusually recent preservation failure after a career that began in British service and continued for decades with the Indian Navy. Dr. Clarke highlights her Falklands service, Cold War career, Centaur-class design, and survival longer than the Invincible-class carriers. He does not insist that the museum had to be in Britain: the video considers both Britain and India credible homes. A petition reached India's Supreme Court, but by the time it succeeded roughly forty percent of the ship had already been dismantled, turning delay into an irreversible preservation failure.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Hermes's British and Indian service, her 2021 scrapping, and failed preservation efforts in both countries.

segments.title: The Tribal destroyers left outside the final three
segments.summary: The strict rules spare Dr. Clarke from choosing among Tartar, Ashanti, Nubian, and Eskimo, four destroyers whose preservation he would personally champion.
segments.body: The video briefly sets the formal list aside to explain why some Royal Navy destroyers would make a much harder personal choice. Dr. Clarke points to surviving representatives HMCS Haida and HMAS Vampire, but regrets that no Battle-class destroyer survives. He singles out Tartar, Ashanti, Nubian, and Eskimo for service records that would each reward a museum treatment. Ashanti's association with the Gold Coast and the unusual amphibious-warfare work connected with Tartar show how these destroyers could interpret colonial relationships, operations, and institutional experimentation as well as ship design.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Tartar, Ashanti, Nubian, Eskimo, existing Tribal and Daring representatives, and the missing Battle class.

segments.title: HMS Ashanti and the Gold Coast connection
segments.summary: Ashanti's commissioning relationship with the Ashanti people creates a preservation story extending beyond Britain and the destroyer's combat record.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke suggests that a preserved HMS Ashanti might appropriately have gone to the Gold Coast rather than remained automatically in Britain. The Ashanti people took pride in the ship at commissioning, held a major ceremony, and presented gifts or prizes for her crew. That relationship would allow a museum to explore how a Royal Navy warship's identity was understood by the community whose name it carried. The example broadens preservation beyond national ownership by asking where a ship's strongest historical and cultural connections lie.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Ashanti pride at commissioning, ceremonial support for the crew, and a possible museum home on the Gold Coast.

segments.title: HMS Tartar as a platform for amphibious experimentation
segments.summary: Tartar's wartime use by a specialist group studying surveillance and landings gives the destroyer an institutional history beyond conventional fleet action.
segments.body: The transcript highlights unusual wartime work aboard HMS Tartar connected with a specialist organization focused on amphibious warfare. Personnel used the destroyer to observe operations and landings and to study how such missions could be carried out. Dr. Clarke cautions that the group was not simply the Special Operations Executive in the familiar sense, even though officers may have used ranks or covers that obscured their seniority. Preserving Tartar would therefore have opened a window onto the experimental organizations and practical observation behind British amphibious methods.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke describes a specialist amphibious-warfare group using Tartar to survey operations and landings, with uncertain cover identities among its officers.

segments.title: Why Illustrious wins the carrier place
segments.summary: The final carrier choice comes down to Illustrious versus Saratoga, with Britain's lack of any preserved carrier deciding the comparison.
segments.body: When forced to choose only one carrier, Dr. Clarke narrows the field to Illustrious and Saratoga rather than Hermes. He concludes that Illustrious has the greater preservation value because the United States already has other museum carriers while Britain has none. This is the video's clearest statement of its broader method: scarcity of representation can outweigh the appeal of an individual ship's story.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke selects Illustrious over Saratoga because Britain has no preserved carrier.

segments.title: Why Tiger wins the capital-ship choice
segments.summary: Tiger beats Gloire, Devastation, Warspite, and Rodney because a surviving battlecruiser would fill the most conspicuous gap in naval museums.
segments.body: The final capital-ship comparison acknowledges the appeal of a Washington Treaty battleship, Warspite's service, Devastation's technological importance, and Gloire's French story. Dr. Clarke nevertheless chooses Tiger because no battlecruiser survives, and because her First World War service makes her a particularly clear representative of the type. He imagines her alongside Victory, Warrior, and Mary Rose at Portsmouth as a continuous museum walk through major stages of British warship history.

evidence.note: The transcript compares the capital-ship candidates and selects Tiger as the missing battlecruiser representative.

segments.title: Why Implacable completes the final three
segments.summary: HMS Implacable is chosen over Great Michael, Carcass, turtle ships, treasure ships, and quinqueremes because she was a known surviving third-rate lost as late as 1949.
segments.body: The last choice separates ships that would settle major archaeological debates from a specific ship whose survival was still within reach. Dr. Clarke selects Implacable because she was a French-built third-rate, had fought at Trafalgar, survived into the Second World War as HMS Foudroyant, and was reportedly the last afloat veteran of that battle. Her 1949 scuttling therefore becomes the sharpest example in the video of a museum opportunity lost despite an unusually well-documented and accessible historic vessel.

evidence.note: The transcript makes the final selection of Implacable, Tiger, and Illustrious and explains Implacable's late survival and Trafalgar connection.

segments.title: Virtual reconstruction as a way to revisit lost ships
segments.summary: When a hull cannot be saved, carefully researched virtual reality could still let visitors move through lost warships and understand their spaces.
segments.body: The closing idea turns the loss of physical ships into a case for virtual reconstruction. Dr. Clarke describes a long-term ambition to build virtual-reality walkthroughs based on books, photographs, and contemporary accounts, so that viewers can explore ships that cannot be preserved or rebuilt. The proposal depends on careful research rather than pure spectacle: the more reliable the source material, the more convincing the imagined ship can become.

evidence.note: The transcript describes a research-led virtual-reality project for walking through lost ships.
